behaviors.description: The conversation history passed to the LLM is trimmed to the most recent 10 messages when the full history exceeds the context window.
behaviors.justification: The real failure boundary is at module wiring: history selection + prompt assembly + LLM client payload. A unit test of just the trim helper can miss ordering/schema bugs where the wrong 10 messages are actually sent.
behaviors.test_description: Run the request handler with an in-memory/fake LLM transport that records outbound payloads. Provide 15 prior messages with distinct IDs/timestamps; assert the LLM call contains exactly the latest 10 in correct order and roles. This catches adapter/payload wiring failures that a pure helper unit test would miss.

behaviors.description: The generated response addresses the customer's question rather than producing a generic or off-topic reply.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on real model judgment/generation quality, not deterministic code paths. Mocking the model would hide the key failure (semantically off-topic answers that still pass format checks).
behaviors.test_description: Execute one real LLM generation step on a curated eval set of customer questions with conversation context; score relevance against rubric/LLM-judge with strict pass criteria (e.g., must reference asked issue and provide task-specific guidance). Fail on generic/off-topic responses. This requires a real model call.

behaviors.description: The tone re-check correctly identifies when a response is too formal for a casual-tier customer and triggers regeneration.
behaviors.justification: The detection step itself relies on LLM judgment of tone. Deterministic mocks can validate branching but not whether real outputs are correctly flagged as too formal/casual.
behaviors.test_description: Use real LLM tone-check prompts on a labeled set of responses (including clearly too-formal examples for casual tier). Assert flagged cases trigger a second generation call and non-flagged cases do not. Validate both classification quality and trigger behavior in one test harness.

behaviors.description: The full exchange (user message, generated response, any regenerations) is persisted to the database with correct timestamps.
behaviors.justification: This is primarily persistence correctness across app code + DB schema/transactions. Unit tests can miss serialization/mapping/timestamp issues that only appear with a real DB interaction.
behaviors.test_description: Run end-to-end request handling against a real local test DB (same ORM/migrations). Force a regeneration path, then assert stored records include user message, first draft, regenerated draft, final served response marker, and monotonic timestamps with expected timezone/precision.

behaviors.description: When the tone check triggers regeneration, the second response replaces the first — the customer never sees the rejected draft.
behaviors.justification: Failure is in orchestration/output plumbing: draft handling, state replacement, and response emission. This is best caught by wiring-level tests with controlled model outputs; no real model judgment is required if trigger is induced deterministically.
behaviors.test_description: Use a deterministic LLM stub sequence: first response intentionally flagged, second acceptable. Invoke the full handler and assert outbound customer payload contains only second response, while first exists only in internal logs/audit fields. Verify no intermediate stream/event leaks the rejected draft.
